name: Winston-Salem/Forsyth County Schools
districtFact: Fourth largest school district in North Carolina, serving the Winston-Salem metropolitan area — home to Wake Forest University, major medical research institutions, and a historic manufacturing heritage.
about: Winston-Salem/Forsyth County Schools serves approximately 55,000 students across 80 schools in Winston-Salem and Forsyth County, North Carolina, in the Piedmont Triad region.
planningTips: WSFCS starts August 10 — two weeks before Charlotte-Mecklenburg (Aug 25). Families coordinating with CMS relatives should note the two-week gap at the start and the one-week gap at spring break.
WSFCS Spring Break (Mar 26–Apr 2) is one week later than Guilford County's (Mar 22–26). The districts overlap only on Good Friday (Mar 26) — plan multi-district family visits accordingly.
Wake Forest University is in Winston-Salem but is completely separate from WSFCS. WFU's semester calendar has no connection to K-12 school dates in Forsyth County.
WSFCS ends May 21 — among the earliest last days in NC. Summer camps and childcare can start earlier than in Wake County (May 24) or Charlotte-Mecklenburg (June 9).

districtFaqs.q: Is Winston-Salem/Forsyth County Schools the same as Wake Forest University?
districtFaqs.a: No. Winston-Salem/Forsyth County Schools (WSFCS) is the K-12 public school system serving Winston-Salem and Forsyth County. Wake Forest University is a separate private research university that happens to be located in Winston-Salem. Despite the shared 'Wake' name, the two institutions are completely unrelated. Wake Forest University's semester schedule has no connection to WSFCS school dates. Wake Forest University relocated from the town of Wake Forest (in Wake County) to Winston-Salem in 1956, which is why the name can cause confusion.

livingHere.intro: Winston-Salem is a mid-sized Piedmont city in the midst of a decades-long economic transition — from its roots as the tobacco and textile capital of the South toward a growing identity anchored by one of the largest medical school complexes in the Southeast and a vibrant arts scene. Families relocating here find lower housing costs than Charlotte or Raleigh, a walkable arts district, and a city with a strong sense of its own distinct identity.

highlights.label: Atrium Health Wake Forest Baptist
highlights.detail: Wake Forest Baptist Medical Center (now part of Atrium Health) is Winston-Salem's largest employer, housing a level-one trauma center and one of the Southeast's leading academic medical research programs — a primary draw for healthcare and research professionals relocating to the area.

highlights.label: Wake Forest University
highlights.detail: Wake Forest University, a private research university with roughly 9,000 students, is located in Winston-Salem's northern suburbs. Its campus events, athletics, and employer partnerships are woven into local life, but the university's calendar is entirely separate from WSFCS school dates.

highlights.label: Reynolds American / BAT Legacy
highlights.detail: R.J. Reynolds Tobacco (now British American Tobacco's US headquarters) shaped Winston-Salem's built environment and philanthropic institutions for over a century. Reynolds endowments funded Reynolda House Museum of American Art and remain a visible part of the city's cultural fabric.

highlights.label: Arts District and SECCA
highlights.detail: The Arts District along Trade Street and the Southeastern Center for Contemporary Art (SECCA) make Winston-Salem one of the more arts-active mid-size cities in the South. The North Carolina School of the Arts (NCSA), a nationally ranked conservatory, is also located here.

highlights.label: Piedmont Triad Airport (PTI/GSO)
highlights.detail: Piedmont Triad International Airport is shared between Winston-Salem and Greensboro, located roughly 20 miles east of downtown. Most major destinations require a connection through Charlotte (CLT) or Atlanta (ATL), making CLT the practical choice for many WSFCS families traveling by air.

highlights.label: High Point Furniture Market Access
highlights.detail: The High Point Market, the world's largest furnishings trade show, is 20 miles south of Winston-Salem. The twice-yearly event (April and October) affects lodging availability and traffic throughout the Piedmont Triad during market weeks.
